channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: F.A.A. Briefly Halts JetBlue Departures After System Outage
item.description: The Federal Aviation Administration issued a ground stop for JetBlue flights early Tuesday at the airline’s request. JetBlue later said a “system outage” had been restored and that operations had resumed.
item.dc:creator: Dan Watson

item.title: Trump Seeks to Calm Oil and Gas Markets but Says Iran War Will Go On
item.description: The president said the U.S. could accompany tankers through the Strait of Hormuz if necessary to keep oil flowing. “We’ve already won in many ways, but we haven’t won enough,” he said.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Benoit Tessier/Reuters
item.media:description: Oil tankers sat anchored in Muscat, Oman, on Saturday as Iran vowed to block the Strait of Hormuz amid the conflict.

item.title: Justice Department Says Trump Can Overrule California and Approve Oil Pipeline
item.description: The Justice Department said the president has the legal authority to overrule state regulators and reopen an offshore oil pipeline that was shut down after causing a gigantic oil spill.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.category: Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Esther oil and gas platform, located off the coast of Seal Beach, Calif., was shut down last year. But the Trump administration hopes to increase oil production elsewhere off the California coast, including at the site of a 2015 oil spill.

item.title: Two Supreme Court Justices Debate Handling of Trump Emergency Cases
item.description: In a rare joint appearance, Justices Ketanji Brown Jackson and Brett M. Kavanaugh offered sharply different views on how the court should handle emergency requests.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Supreme Court Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson, left, moderator Judge Paul L. Friedman, center, and Supreme Court Justice Brett M. Kavanaugh on Monday, where the two justices differed over how the court is handling emergency requests.

item.title: Trump’s War in Iran, and Rising Gas Prices, Collide With Midterm Agenda
item.description: The attack on Iran has led to a surge in energy prices at a moment when the cost of living is a major issue heading into the fall elections.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Benoit Tessier/Reuters
item.media:description: A tanker and a cargo ship in Muscat, Oman, on Monday. The war in Iran has affected the shipping of oil through the Strait of Hormuz and the Gulf of Oman.

item.title: Trump Again Suggests Without Evidence That Iran Struck Elementary School
item.description: After a video showed a Tomahawk missile hitting a naval base next to the school, President Trump claimed Iran possesses such weapons. But the U.S. developed the missile and has sold it only to a few close allies.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh and John Ismay
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The president suggested without giving evidence on Monday that Iran or another country could have fired the weapon.

item.title: Murder Charge Is Dropped Against Man Who Fired Gun at Chiefs Super Bowl Parade
item.description: Dominic M. Miller, 20, was sentenced to two years in jail as part of a plea agreement related to the 2024 killing of Lisa Lopez-Galvan. He has already served most of his time.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Dominick Williams for The New York Times
item.media:description: The violence at a 2024 Super Bowl event in Kansas City, Mo., wounded two dozen people, including nine children.

item.title: Epstein’s Remote Zorro Ranch Is Searched by New Mexico Investigators
item.description: Some of the disgraced financier’s victims have said they were trafficked at the property south of Santa Fe, where the nearest neighbors are miles away.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lawmakers voted to impanel a bipartisan four-member “truth commission” with subpoena power to investigate Zorro Ranch.

item.title: Iran’s Women’s Soccer Team Offered Asylum by President Trump
item.description: The dissonance between the president’s hard-line immigration policies and his offer of asylum to the athletes was striking. Australia took in five players.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:credit: Dave Hunt/Australian Associated Press, via Reuters
item.media:description: Members of the Iranian women’s soccer team after a match on Australia’s Gold Coast on Sunday.

item.title: Iran War Overshadows Midterms as Democrats Focus on Economy
item.description: Democrats are focused on the economy, while Republicans wish they were focused on the economy.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: An explosion after an airstrike in Tehran on Saturday.

item.title: 2 Teen Mariachi Musicians Released From ICE Detention
item.description: Representative Joaquin Castro of Texas led a delegation of Democrats to a South Texas detention center to press for the release of the brothers and their family.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Joaquin Castro, Democrat of Texas, greeting Caleb Gámez-Cuéllar and his family after their release from the Dilley Immigration Processing Center in Dilley, Texas, on Monday.

item.title: U.S. Reaches Tentative Deal Ending Prosecution of Turkish Bank
item.description: Court papers submitted as part of the agreement with Halkbank, which was accused of doing business with Iranian entities, said Turkey’s assistance “was instrumental” in the Israel-Hamas cease-fire.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Umit Bektas/Reuters
item.media:description: President Recep Tayyip Erdogan of Turkey has long pressed President Trump to quash the case against the Turkish bank.

item.title: Alexander Butterfield, Who Revealed Nixon Tapes in Watergate Scandal, Dies at 99
item.description: “There is tape in the Oval Office,” said Mr. Butterfield, a former White House aide, in testimony that rocked the Watergate hearings and led to the president’s resignation.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: Alexander P. Butterfield testifying before the Senate Watergate Committee in July 1973. He revealed to the American public the existence of a secret recording system that President Richard M. Nixon had authorized and that had picked up virtually all of his meetings and telephone conversations.

item.title: Casey Wasserman Agency Removes His Name From Company in Epstein Fallout
item.description: The sports and marketing agency is contending with the fallout from the Epstein files, and it is continuing to look for a buyer.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler and Nicole Stock
item.category: Los Angeles Organizing Committee for the Olympic and Paralympic Games 2028 (LA28)
item.media:credit: Guglielmo Mangiapane/Reuters
item.media:description: Casey Wasserman suffered a reputational blow early this year after files released by the Justice Department included suggestive emails he sent years ago to the longtime companion of Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: Locals in Other States Prepare for ICE With Lots of Rumors and Little Information
item.description: After the sprawling and chaotic federal immigration crackdown in Minnesota, jurisdictions in other states have been bracing for a range of scenarios.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: The clashes during immigration sweeps in Minneapolis earlier this year have left local activists and officials in other cities worried that their communities could face similar crackdowns.

item.title: Judge Halts Trump Administration Move to Restrict Immigration Appeals
item.description: The ruling is part of a broader dispute between the independent federal judiciary and the executive branch’s immigration court system.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: Todd Heisler/The New York Times
item.media:description: 26 Federal Plaza in Manhattan, where detained immigrants attend their court hearings.

item.title: As Kari Lake Sought to Shutter Voice of America, Parent Agency Rebuffed Auditors
item.description: A new report says the lack of information provided for a required annual examination was so “pervasive” that auditors declined to opine on the agency’s financial numbers.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.category: United States Agency for Global Media
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kari Lake has vowed to shut down federally funded newsrooms that she accused of being anti-American and “rotten.”

item.title: Bernard Lafayette Jr., 85, Dies; Civil Rights Leader Helped Plan Selma March
item.description: A close associate of Martin Luther King Jr. and John Lewis, he was involved in many of the key moments of the Black freedom struggle in the 1960s.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Lee Lockwood/WSPI, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Bernard Lafayette Jr. during a planning session in Montgomery, Ala., in 1961. Dr. Lafayette played a key role in some of the central events of the civil rights movement.

item.title: O.S.U. President Walter Carter Jr. Resigns Over ‘Inappropriate Relationship’
item.description: The university said it was investigating a company owned by a podcaster after the president, Walter Carter Jr., said he had “made a mistake.”
item.dc:creator: Vimal Patel
item.media:credit: Jason Mowry/Getty Images
item.media:description: Ohio State University president Walter Carter Jr. at Ohio Stadium in Columbus last year.

item.title: Hip-Hop Icons Tell Justices That Texas Turned Rap Lyrics Into a Death Warrant
item.description: Killer Mike, Travis Scott, T.I. and other artists said James Broadnax was sent to death row in Texas based partly on his artistic expression.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: “It’s an interpretation of the human spirit, said Killer Mike, the performer and political activist, about James Broadnax’s lyrics. “It is not an admission of guilt.”

item.title: New Lawsuit Challenges Rubio’s Threats Against Foreign Tech Regulators
item.description: A lawsuit filed on Monday argues that a State Department’s decision to withhold visas from experts who have pushed for stronger social media regulations is illegal.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced last year that he was restricting visas from foreign nationals whose calls for global content moderation policies, he said, amounted to “flagrant censorship actions.”

item.title: Trump Threatens to Crowd Out Republicans’ Midterm Message
item.description: As the G.O.P. gathered in Miami for a party retreat where lawmakers hoped to focus on the economy, the president threatened to block his own party’s legislative agenda.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Polls suggest that Republicans’ chances of keeping control of the House are growing more slim.

item.title: Pennsylvania State Trooper Is Fatally Shot During a Traffic Stop
item.description: A driver opened fired on Pennsylvania State Police Cpl. Tim O’Connor before killing himself on Sunday night, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.media:credit: 6ABC
item.media:description: The scene of the shooting in West Caln Township, Pa.

item.title: How Markwayne Mullin Became Trump’s Homeland Security Pick
item.description: The Oklahoma senator whom President Trump chose to run the Department of Homeland Security has risen in Washington by styling himself as a MAGA warrior and Trump loyalist.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: If Mr. Mullin were to be confirmed by the Senate, he would take the mantle of a federal agency carrying out the president’s immigration agenda.

item.title: 4 Takeaways on How Billionaires Are Donating to Federal Elections
item.description: 300 billionaires and their families spent more than $3 billion on federal elections in 2024. Here’s a snapshot of how a surging class of ultrawealthy people is spending money on political change.
item.dc:creator: Steven Rich and Mike Baker
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie/The New York Times
item.media:description: Before the Supreme Court’s 2010 ruling that lifted many remaining campaign finance restrictions, the share of billionaire spending on federal elections was quite small.

item.title: James Talarico Challenges ‘Heretical’ Right-Wing Christianity in Texas Senate Race
item.description: James Talarico, the Democratic nominee from Texas, hopes to counter what he sees as a conservative takeover of the American church.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Elizabeth Dias
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Talarico has cast his Senate candidacy as an opportunity for Christians to reclaim what he sees as core principals of their faith in American political life.

item.title: Voting Rights Groups Raise Alarms About Case Before the Supreme Court
item.description: The groups are working to educate voters in the South about how they would be affected if the court strikes down a key provision of the Voting Rights Act.
item.dc:creator: Audra D. S. Burch and Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Rita Harper for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Rev. Al Sharpton said that this year’s march in Selma, Ala., was about continuing, rather than commemorating, the fight to protect voting rights.

item.title: Bennie Thompson Faces a Young Challenger in the Mississippi Primary
item.description: The longest-serving Black Democrat in Mississippi, Bennie Thompson is a formidable political force. But he is not immune to a national trend of younger candidates going after older incumbents.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Bennie Thompson, Democrat of Mississippi, was first elected to Congress in 1993. He now faces a primary challenge from a much younger opponent.

item.title: Billionaires Made 19% of Federal Election Campaign Contributions in 2024
item.description: Billionaires made 19 percent of all reported federal campaign contributions in 2024, a Times analysis shows, and even more in some local elections. Wealthy donors are reaping the rewards.
item.dc:creator: Mike Baker and Steven Rich
item.category: Citizens United v Federal Election Commission (Supreme Court Decision)
item.media:credit: Win McNamee/Getty Images
item.media:description: Protesters demonstrated outside the Supreme Court in 2019 as Senate Democrats proposed a constitutional amendment to overturn the court’s decision that lifted many campaign finance restrictions.

item.title: His Mother Vanished When He Was 14. 33 Years Later, He Found Her.
item.description: Antonio Wiley’s mother, Anita, vanished during a time when her hometown Detroit was wracked by drugs and crime. It took three decades and an extraordinary effort by a missing persons detective to locate her.
item.dc:creator: Chris Hippensteel
item.media:credit: Nic Antaya for The New York Times
item.media:description: Antonio Wiley at the gravesite of his mother, Anita Wiley, who died in 1987.
